title: Document Feature Extraction
concept: To classify text, we convert normal strings into numeric features using TF-IDF or Bag of Words before feeding them to classifiers.

exercises.prompt: Do we need to convert text to numbers before giving it to a machine learning classifier?

options.text: Yes, computers need numeric inputs to calculate.
No, computers can read text just like humans do.
Only if the text is in English.

exercises.explain: Computers operate on numbers. To classify text, we must first convert words into numeric values.

cards.title: Computers Can't Read!
cards.body: AI models are math wizards, but they are terrible at reading books! They cannot understand words like 'happy' or 'pizza' directly. How do we teach a computer to classify text if it only understands numbers? We turn text into a math puzzle!

cards.title: Document Feature Extraction
cards.body: Document Feature Extraction is the process of converting raw text sentences into lists of numbers. Because computers only understand numbers, we represent text as word counts or importance scores so machine learning models can classify them.

cards.title: The Word Counter
cards.body: Imagine you are sorting books in a library, but you don't speak the language. Instead of reading, you just count how many times words like 'planet', 'rocket', and 'star' appear. By counting these words, you can easily group all the space books together without reading a single sentence!

cards.title: From Words to Numbers
cards.body:
If we have the sentence "Cat chases mouse," a Bag of Words tool might turn it into a table of counts:
• cat: 1
• chases: 1
• mouse: 1
• dog: 0

This list of numbers [1, 1, 1, 0] is the feature vector that our classifier can easily read!

cards.title: Does Order Matter?
cards.body: People often think that feature extraction keeps the exact order and grammar of your sentences. In reality, methods like Bag of Words completely throw away the grammar and word order, yet they still work amazingly well for finding the main topic!

cards.title: Text vs. Numbers
cards.body: See how the computer simplifies raw human language into structured numbers that a classifier can compute.

left.text: "I love coding and I love AI."

exercises.prompt: How does a 'Bag of Words' model extract features from a document?

options.text: It counts how many times each word appears in the text.
It checks the grammar and spelling of the text.
It translates the text into another language.
It deletes all the vowels from the sentences.

exercises.explain: A Bag of Words model represents text by counting how often each word appears, ignoring the grammar and the order in which they were written.

exercises.prompt: True or False: TF-IDF is a method used to convert text into numeric features by weighing word importance.
exercises.explain: TF-IDF is a feature extraction method that gives higher weight to unique, important words and lower weight to common words like 'the' or 'is'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the core concept of feature extraction.
exercises.sentence: To train a text classifier, we must convert raw text into lists of numbers.
exercises.explain: Because machine learning models are mathematical, we must convert raw text strings into numbers (numerical features) before training.

exercises.prompt: Which statement is true about text classifiers?

options.text: Classifiers process raw text strings directly without any conversion.
Classifiers require numeric data, so we must extract features first.
Classifiers only accept images as input, not numbers.

exercises.explain: Standard machine learning classifiers (like Naive Bayes or SVMs) cannot process raw, unstructured text strings directly. They require numeric vectors.

exercises.prompt: Why is it absolutely necessary to use a technique like Bag of Words or TF-IDF before sending text to a classifier model?

options.text: Classifiers are smart enough to read human paragraphs directly without any conversion.
We must convert the text into numeric features so the machine learning model can process it.
We convert text to numbers because numbers take up more computer screen space.
We only convert text to numbers to translate it into different human languages.

exercises.explain: Computers cannot directly read sentences to make decisions; they require text to be transformed into numeric features (like word counts or TF-IDF scores) before a classifier model can process them.
